The gods and demons, terrified by the poison, sought refuge in Brahma, who then directed them to Shiva. Shiva consumed the poison, saving the universe, and the poison left a blue mark on his throat.

story.title: The Origin of Shiva's Blue Throat
story.arc: Shiva explains to Parvati how he came to have a blue mark on his throat after drinking the poison Halahala during the churning of the ocean.
story.timeline_note: Set during the churning of the ocean (Samudra Manthana), a primordial event.
story.comic_potential: High; the dramatic emergence of poison, the gods' panic, and Shiva's heroic act of drinking it are visually striking.

teachings.teaching: Shiva's consumption of poison symbolizes the yogi's ability to assimilate and transcend negativity through inner purification.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the advanced practitioner's capacity to neutralize the poison of karmic impressions (samskaras) through meditative absorption, turning them into spiritual nectar.

teachings.teaching: The gods and demons working together yet being terrified by the poison illustrates the dual nature of worldly pursuits.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this as the cooperation of opposing forces (prana and apana) in the body, which can produce both beneficial and harmful energies; the yogi must learn to manage them.

teachings.teaching: Brahma's reassurance that the gods are lords of the three worlds and should not fear highlights the importance of recognizing one's inherent power.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the Self as the witness (sakshi) beyond the play of gunas, which dispels fear.

chapter_summary: Brahma praises Shiva with a long hymn describing his various forms and attributes, then requests Shiva to consume the poison Halahala that emerged from the churning of the ocean, as only Shiva can withstand its power.

story.title: Brahma's Hymn to Shiva and the Request to Drink the Poison
story.arc: Brahma praises Shiva with a detailed hymn, then asks him to drink the poison Halahala that emerged from the churning of the ocean to protect the worlds.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the churning of the ocean (Samudra Manthana) when the poison Halahala appears.
story.comic_potential: High; dramatic scene of Brahma's elaborate praise and the urgent request to Shiva to consume the deadly poison.

teachings.teaching: Shiva is the supreme being who can withstand and consume the most dangerous poison for the welfare of the worlds.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the yogi's ability to assimilate and transform negative experiences (poison) into spiritual energy through inner discipline and surrender to the divine.

teachings.teaching: The hymn describes Shiva as both immanent and transcendent, encompassing all opposites (nitya-anitya, vyakta-avyakta, etc.).
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of integrating dualities through meditation, realizing the unity of all existence.

chapter_summary: Shiva recounts to Parvati the story of how he became Nīlakaṇṭha by drinking the poison Kālakūṭa during the churning of the ocean, and describes the benefits of reciting the Brahmodya hymn.

story.title: The Origin of Nīlakaṇṭha
story.arc: Shiva recounts to Parvati how he drank the poison Kālakūṭa during the churning of the ocean, turning his throat blue, and the subsequent praise by the gods.
story.timeline_note: Set after the churning of the ocean (Samudra Manthana).
story.comic_potential: The dramatic moment of drinking poison and the gods' astonishment, with vivid descriptions of Takṣaka coiling around Shiva's neck.

teachings.teaching: Reciting the Brahmodya hymn (brahmodgītā) daily brings protection from poison, auspiciousness, victory, and prosperity.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes the power of sound (nāda) and mantra to transform consciousness and neutralize negative influences, aligning with the practice of japa and meditation on the divine name.

teachings.teaching: Shiva as Nīlakaṇṭha is the supreme reality, identical to all deities and elements.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views this as a non-dual teaching: the practitioner realizes the unity of all existence in the Self, which is Śiva.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Brahmanda Purana describes the glory of a stotra (hymn) dedicated to Shiva, detailing its benefits for various beings, and then transitions to a narrative where the gods praise Vishnu after his victory over Bali. Vishnu reveals that he is not the ultimate creator but serves a higher power, and recounts a vision of a four-faced being (Brahma) emerging from the cosmic waters.

story.title: The Glory of the Shiva Stotra and Vishnu's Revelation
story.arc: The chapter first extols the benefits of reciting a stotra to Shiva, then shifts to a narrative where the gods praise Vishnu after his victory over Bali. Vishnu humbly reveals that he is not the ultimate creator but serves a higher power, and recounts a vision of Brahma emerging from the cosmic waters.
story.timeline_note: The stotra section is timeless; the narrative occurs after Vishnu's victory over Bali, which is a post-Vamana avatar event.
story.comic_potential: The contrast between the gods' praise of Vishnu and Vishnu's humble revelation of a higher power could be depicted with dramatic irony.

teachings.teaching: Reciting or even partially memorizing this stotra leads to liberation (Rudraloka) and worldly benefits.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the power of sound and intention (mantra) to transform consciousness and align with divine will.

teachings.teaching: Vishnu declares that he is not the ultimate creator but serves a higher power (Brahma) who is the source of all.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of surrendering the ego and recognizing a transcendent reality beyond even the highest deities.

chapter_summary: Brahma and Vishnu argue over who is supreme, then see a massive fiery pillar (linga). They try to find its ends, fail, and realize Shiva is the ultimate reality, praising him.

story.title: The Origin of the Linga and the Supremacy of Shiva
story.arc: Brahma and Vishnu's dispute resolved by Shiva's manifestation as a fiery pillar, leading to their surrender and praise.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after creation, before the full establishment of the Trimurti hierarchy.
story.comic_potential: Two gods arguing like children, then running up and down a giant pillar for a thousand years, only to give up and sing praises.

teachings.teaching: Shiva is the ultimate reality beyond all forms and attributes, the source of all mantras and rituals.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the supreme Self (Atman) as the witness of all actions, achieved through disciplined practice and surrender of ego.

teachings.teaching: The linga represents the formless, infinite nature of the divine, beyond comprehension.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret the linga as the symbol of the unmanifest (avyakta) which is the goal of meditation; the failure to find its ends signifies the limit of intellectual understanding.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Brahmanda Purana is a hymn of praise to Lord Shiva, describing his cosmic functions and manifestations. It identifies Shiva as the source of all elements, deities, and cosmic principles, and narrates his appearance before Brahma and Vishnu, who are born from his arms. Shiva grants them a boon of devotion and the ability to create progeny.

story.title: The Hymn of Praise to Shiva and His Manifestation
story.arc: Cosmic theophany and boon granting
story.timeline_note: Occurs in a timeless cosmic setting before creation.
story.comic_potential: High: visual of Shiva with thousand faces and cosmic forms, dramatic appearance and boon.

teachings.teaching: Shiva is the source of all elements, deities, and cosmic principles; he is the ultimate reality.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the Self as the source of all creation, achieved through disciplined meditation and pranayama.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Shiva leads to liberation and the fulfillment of desires.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that unwavering devotion (bhakti) combined with kriya-yoga purifies the mind and leads to direct experience of the divine.

chapter_summary: The sages ask Suta to narrate the story of how Lord Shiva, in the Daruvana forest, assumed a strange form to test and bless the sages. Suta recounts that in the Krita Yuga, many sages performed severe austerities in the Devadaruvana forest. To grant them grace, Shiva appeared as a naked, ash-smeared, wild-looking ascetic, carrying a firebrand, with a red phallus and scrotum, laughing, singing, dancing, and frightening the sages' wives. He then began to deceive and make everyone laugh.

story.title: Shiva's Strange Appearance in Daruvana
story.arc: The sages ask Suta to narrate how Shiva assumed a strange form in Daruvana forest to test and bless the sages. Suta recounts the story from the Krita Yuga.
story.timeline_note: Set in Krita Yuga, after the previous chapter's events.
story.comic_potential: High - Shiva's bizarre, naked, ash-smeared appearance, laughing, singing, dancing, and frightening the sages' wives has strong comedic and absurd elements.

teachings.teaching: The supreme knowledge of Shiva is subtle and inconceivable, seen only by the eye of wisdom.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this points to the direct realization of the Self through inner yogic practice, beyond intellectual understanding.

teachings.teaching: Reciting the hymn to Shiva grants all desires and frees from sins.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on devotional practice (bhakti) as a means to purify the mind and attain grace.

teachings.teaching: Shiva's strange form and actions are a means to test and bless devotees, demonstrating that the divine can manifest in unconventional ways.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this teaches non-attachment to external forms and the importance of inner recognition of the divine beyond appearances.

chapter_summary: The sages, deluded by Shiva's maya, curse him in anger, but their curses prove ineffective. They then realize their error and ask Shiva to abandon his linga form. Shiva refuses, stating that even Brahma and other gods cannot force him, but he himself can. He then disappears, causing cosmic imbalance: the sun loses heat, fire loses luster, stars and planets become reversed, and the sages' rituals fail. The sages become devoid of energy and their dharma wavers.

story.title: The Sages' Curse on Shiva and Its Aftermath
story.arc: Shiva's maya deludes the sages, leading to a curse that backfires, causing cosmic disturbance and the sages' loss of power.
story.timeline_note: This episode occurs after the sages are deluded by Shiva's maya; it references past curses by other sages.
story.comic_potential: The sages' futile anger and Shiva's calm disappearance, followed by the absurd cosmic chaos, has a darkly humorous tone.

teachings.teaching: Curses from deluded minds are ineffective against the divine; only self-realization can overcome ignorance.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This illustrates that external actions (curses) without inner purification (kriya) are powerless. True transformation comes from aligning with the divine will through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: When the divine principle (Shiva as linga) is withdrawn, the cosmos loses its order and vitality.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: The linga symbolizes the subtle body's core energy. Its withdrawal represents loss of pranic balance, leading to spiritual stagnation. Kriya-Yoga restores this inner fire.

chapter_summary: The sages, having lost their spiritual power and luster, approach Brahma in Brahmaloka to seek guidance after insulting a strange being (Shiva in disguise). Brahma reveals that the being is Mahadeva, the supreme lord, and instructs them to worship his linga form. The sages then go to Devadaru forest and begin a year-long penance. At the end of the year, Shiva appears, and the sages praise him and ask for forgiveness.

story.title: The Sages' Penance and Shiva's Grace
story.arc: The sages, having insulted Shiva, seek Brahma's advice, perform penance, and are forgiven by Shiva.
story.timeline_note: After the sages lost their powers, they went to Brahma, then performed penance for one year in Devadaru forest.
story.comic_potential: The sages' initial arrogance and subsequent humble penance, with Shiva's dramatic appearance, offers a classic redemption arc.

teachings.teaching: Shiva is the supreme lord, beyond all, and should be worshipped with controlled senses and anger.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the need for inner purification and surrender to the divine will as the highest practice.

teachings.teaching: The linga is a representation of Shiva's form and should be worshipped to attain his vision and destroy ignorance and adharma.
teachings.lens_note: The linga symbolizes the formless aspect of the divine, and its worship in Kriya-Yoga is a meditative focus to transcend duality.

chapter_summary: The sages praise Lord Shiva, describing his various forms and attributes, and seek his grace. They acknowledge that all actions, whether done knowingly or unknowingly, are performed by the Lord through his Yogamaya. They request to see him as before and extol his supremacy as the ultimate reality.

story.title: The Sages' Hymn to Shiva
story.arc: The sages, having performed penance, praise Shiva and seek his vision, acknowledging his supremacy and the illusory nature of actions.
story.timeline_note: This hymn is part of a larger dialogue where sages extol Shiva's glories.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic enumeration of Shiva's forms and the sages' fervent praise could be visually striking.

teachings.teaching: All actions, whether done knowingly or unknowingly, are performed by the Lord through his Yogamaya.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that the individual self is not the doer; all actions are the play of divine consciousness. The practitioner should surrender the sense of doership to the divine.

teachings.teaching: Shiva is the ultimate reality, manifesting as all beings and elements.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga view that the same consciousness pervades all forms. Meditation on Shiva as the inner self leads to realization of unity.

teachings.teaching: Desires like kama, krodha, lobha, etc., are to be restrained with the grace of the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that these are obstacles to inner stillness. Through disciplined practice and divine grace, one can overcome them.

chapter_summary: The sages praise Mahadeva and seek to understand the significance of bhasma (sacred ash), nudity, and left-handed practices. Shiva explains that bhasma represents the union of fire and soma, purifies all beings, and those devoted to bhasma are dear to him. He instructs the sages to follow these practices for liberation.

story.title: The Glory of Bhasma and the Instructions to Sages
story.arc: The sages, having performed penance, are granted a vision of Maheśvara. They praise him and ask about the significance of bhasma and related practices. Maheśvara explains the purifying power of bhasma and instructs them to follow the path of bhasma-dharma.
story.timeline_note: This episode occurs after the sages have established the liṅga and are practicing varṇadharma.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic revelation of Maheśvara and the sages' earnest questioning about esoteric practices could be depicted with vivid imagery of fire, ash, and divine light.

teachings.teaching: Bhasma (sacred ash) is the supreme purifier; it represents the union of fire and soma and destroys all sins.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, bhasma symbolizes the inner fire of tapas that burns karmic seeds, and the ash is the residual purity of the self. The practice of applying bhasma is a Kriya technique to invoke the transformative power of Shiva.

teachings.teaching: Those who are devoted to bhasma, follow the prescribed conduct, and are disciplined are dear to Shiva; they should not be criticized or transgressed.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the external practice of bhasma must be accompanied by inner discipline (dānta) and meditation (dhyāna). The Kriya-Yoga practitioner internalizes the ash as the residue of burnt ego.

teachings.teaching: Shiva is both fire and soma, and the world is repeatedly burned by fire; bhasma is the means to achieve purity and liberation.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga concept of the inner fire (agni) and the cooling nectar (soma) as dual aspects of consciousness. The practice of bhasma-snāna is a symbolic act of burning impurities and bathing in the ash of transcendence.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Brahmanda Purana discusses the power and significance of sacred ash (bhasma) in spiritual practice, particularly in the Pashupata tradition. It describes how applying bhasma purifies one from sins, leads to liberation, and grants supernatural powers. The chapter also touches on the nature of the self, the importance of controlling the senses, and the path to Rudra's realm. It includes a dialogue between a sage and Suta about King Pururavas and the lunar cycle.

story.title: The Power of Sacred Ash and the Path to Liberation
story.arc: The chapter begins with a discourse on the purifying power of bhasma, explaining its origin from Agni and Soma, and its role in the Pashupata vow. It then transitions to a dialogue about King Pururavas and the lunar cycle, linking the practice to cosmic rhythms.
story.timeline_note: The chapter is part of the Brahmanda Purana, likely composed between 400-1000 CE. The narrative is timeless, focusing on eternal spiritual principles.
story.comic_potential: The vivid descriptions of bhasma's power and the cosmic dialogue could be visually striking, especially the imagery of ash consuming sins like fire.

teachings.teaching: Bhasma (sacred ash) purifies all sins when applied with faith and used in ritual bathing.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, bhasma symbolizes the ash of burnt karmas; applying it represents the destruction of past impressions through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: The universe is composed of Agni (fire) and Soma (moon), representing the dual principles of heat and coolness, which are manifestations of Rudra.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as the interplay of prana and apana, the two vital energies that must be balanced in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: One who bathes in bhasma with controlled senses and mind attains liberation and does not return.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga goal of achieving kaivalya (isolation) through inner purification and meditation.

teachings.teaching: The Pashupata vow is the highest and was created first; other ashramas were created later.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the Pashupata path is a direct, intense practice suitable for those ready to transcend societal norms.

teachings.teaching: True covering is not cloth but qualities like forgiveness, patience, non-violence, and equanimity.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this points to the inner state of detachment and purity, which is the real protection.

teachings.teaching: Meditating on Mahadeva with bhasma leads to supernatural powers (siddhis) and immortality.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma views siddhis as natural outcomes of deep meditation, but warns against attachment to them.

teachings.teaching: Reciting this chapter with faith purifies sins and leads to Rudraloka.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the power of scripture as a tool for spiritual elevation in the Kriya-Yoga tradition.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Brahmanda Purana explains the lunar cycle, the waxing and waning of the moon, and how the moon provides nectar (amrita) to the pitris (ancestors) during the dark fortnight. It describes the different classes of pitris (Saumya, Barhishada, Agnisvatta, Kavya) and their relationship with the moon, seasons, and time cycles. The story of King Pururavas performing monthly shraddha to satisfy the pitris is narrated.

story.title: The Lunar Cycle and the Satisfaction of the Pitris
story.arc: Cosmological explanation of the moon's phases and the nourishment of ancestors
story.timeline_note: Mythical time; Pururavas is a king of the lunar dynasty.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily didactic and ritualistic.

teachings.teaching: The moon waxes and wanes due to the consumption of its nectar by the gods and pitris.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the inner cycle of pranic energy: the mind (moon) is depleted by outward senses (gods) and replenished by inward focus (shraddha).

teachings.teaching: Performing shraddha on amavasya (new moon) satisfies the pitris.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the practice of honoring one's lineage and ancestors through disciplined action (kriya), which strengthens the subtle body and connects one to the cosmic order.

teachings.teaching: The pitris are classified into three types: Kavya, Barhishada, and Agnisvatta, corresponding to different levels of spiritual attainment.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, these represent stages of purification: Kavya (intellectual), Barhishada (ritualistic), Agnisvatta (fiery transformation).

teachings.teaching: Time cycles (years, seasons, months) are personified as deities and linked to the pitris.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma sees this as a map of the subtle calendar of the body: the breath cycles (prana) correspond to cosmic time, and aligning with them brings harmony.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Brahmanda Purana describes the lunar cycle, including the waxing and waning of the moon, the concept of amavasya (new moon) and purnima (full moon), the role of the sun in replenishing the moon, and the timing of various parvas (junctures) for rituals. It explains how the moon is consumed by the gods and ancestors, and how it is restored by the sun through the suṣumṇā channel.

story.title: The Lunar Cycle and Ritual Timings
story.arc: Cosmological explanation of the moon's phases and their significance for rituals.
story.timeline_note: Describes the monthly cycle of the moon, focusing on the transitions between fortnights.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily technical and ritualistic.

teachings.teaching: The moon is consumed by gods and ancestors during the dark fortnight and replenished by the sun through the suṣumṇā channel.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, suṣumṇā is the central energy channel. The moon's waning and waxing symbolize the flow of prana (life force) through the suṣumṇā, with the sun representing the inner fire that revitalizes the subtle body.

teachings.teaching: The parvas (junctures) like amavasya and purnima are times for ritual offerings, as they mark transitions in the lunar cycle.
teachings.lens_note: These junctures correspond to critical points in the breath cycle (e.g., between inhalation and exhalation) where the mind can be stilled for meditation, as emphasized in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Vyatipata is a specific conjunction of sun and moon considered inauspicious for certain rites.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, such conjunctions represent imbalances in the subtle energies; awareness of these times helps the practitioner avoid distractions.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Brahmanda Purana describes the astronomical and ritual significance of lunar phases, particularly amavasya (new moon) and purnima (full moon), and their connection to the sun and moon. It explains the measurement of time through tithis, the classification of pitrs (ancestors) based on their sacrificial practices, and the importance of shraddha rituals.

story.title: The Science of Lunar Phases and Ancestral Rites
story.arc: Exposition on time measurement and ritual classification
story.timeline_note: Cosmic and ritual time, no narrative sequence
story.comic_potential: Low; technical description of tithis and pitr categories.

teachings.teaching: Amavasya (new moon) is the time when the moon enters the sun's orb, and it is a parva (junction) for rituals.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the merging of individual consciousness (moon) with universal consciousness (sun), a moment for inner stillness and offering.

teachings.teaching: The moon waxes and wanes through fifteen kalas (digits), and on purnima it is full; the sixteenth kala is eternal.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret the sixteen kalas as the sixteen aspects of the self; the sixteenth is the immutable Atman, realized through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: Pitrs are classified into four types: Somapah, Barhishadah, Agnishvattah, and Sayujyagah, based on their sacrificial practices.
teachings.lens_note: This classification reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle that one's spiritual attainments determine their post-mortem state; disciplined action (kriya) leads to higher realms.

teachings.teaching: Shraddha rituals for ancestors are performed with faith and are one of seven paths (tapas, brahmacharya, yajna, praja, shraddha, vidya, dana).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that these seven paths are forms of kriya-yoga; shraddha (faith) is the foundation for all spiritual practice.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Brahmanda Purana describes the fate of souls after death, categorizing them based on their karma. It explains the process of śrāddha offerings, how they reach the pitṛs (ancestors) in various states, and the importance of performing them with faith and proper mantras. The chapter also discusses the different classes of pitṛs and the cyclical relationship between gods and ancestors.

story.title: The Fate of Ancestors and the Efficacy of Śrāddha
story.arc: Exposition on the afterlife and ritual obligations
story.timeline_note: Cosmic time; describes post-mortem states
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic and ritualistic

teachings.teaching: Śrāddha offerings performed with faith and proper mantras reach the ancestors regardless of their state.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the power of intentional action (kriya) and mantra to transcend karmic limitations, aligning with the principle that disciplined practice can purify and elevate the soul.

teachings.teaching: The ancestors are of various types: some enjoy heavenly realms, others suffer in hells, and some are reborn in lower species.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework views these states as results of one's karmic tendencies; the practice of śrāddha serves as a means to generate positive karma that can aid even those in difficult states.

teachings.teaching: The relationship between gods and ancestors is reciprocal; they are mutually dependent.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the interconnectedness of all beings in Sharma's view, where ritual actions maintain cosmic harmony and support spiritual evolution.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Brahmanda Purana describes the cosmic time scales, including the definitions of human, pitrya (ancestral), and divine years, and their interrelationships. It explains the divisions of yugas and the measurement of time from the smallest unit (nimesha) to the largest (divya varsha). The sage asks about the yugas of the Svayambhuva manvantara, and Suta responds by detailing the time calculations.

story.title: Cosmic Time Measurement
story.arc: Exposition of time scales from human to divine
story.timeline_note: The chapter discusses the time measurements of the Svayambhuva manvantara, which is the first manvantara.
story.comic_potential: Low; it is a technical exposition of time units.

teachings.teaching: Time is measured in hierarchical units: nimesha, kashtha, kala, muhurta, day-night, month, year, and divine years. The divine year is 360 human years, and the pitrya year is 30 human years.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this hierarchy reflects the progressive refinement of consciousness. The smallest unit (nimesha) corresponds to the moment of awareness, and the divine year represents the eternal present.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Brahmanda Purana describes the calculation of yuga durations, including sandhya and sandhyamsha periods, and explains the total duration of a manvantara. It also mentions the establishment of dharma by Manu and the seven sages at the beginning of Treta Yuga.

story.title: Yuga and Manvantara Calculations
story.arc: Exposition of cosmic time cycles
story.timeline_note: Describes the beginning of Treta Yuga and the establishment of dharma.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily numerical and doctrinal exposition.

teachings.teaching: The four yugas (Krita, Treta, Dvapara, Kali) have specific durations including sandhya and sandhyamsha periods, totaling 12,000 divine years.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the cyclical nature of time and the importance of aligning practice with cosmic rhythms.

teachings.teaching: A manvantara consists of 71 caturyugas, with a total duration of 30,67,20,000 human years.
teachings.lens_note: This vast timescale underscores the need for persistent spiritual practice across many lifetimes, a key tenet of Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: At the beginning of Treta Yuga, Manu and the seven sages established shrauta and smarta dharma as per Brahma's instruction.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views this as the origin of the dual paths of ritual (shrauta) and conduct (smarta), both essential for spiritual evolution.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Brahmanda Purana describes the origin and transmission of dharma, the role of Manu and the Saptarshis in establishing the Vedic mantras and Smarta dharma, the characteristics of Treta Yuga, the emergence of kingship, and the duties of the four varnas.

story.title: The Origin of Dharma and Kingship in Treta Yuga
story.arc: Cosmic history of dharma transmission from Manu to kings
story.timeline_note: Set in the early Treta Yuga, after the first kalpa, with references to previous kalpas.
story.comic_potential: The scene of Manu meditating and producing two sons, Priyavrata and Uttanapada, who become the first kings, could be depicted as a dramatic moment of divine creation.

teachings.teaching: Dharma is transmitted through parampara (tradition) and is characterized by conduct (acara).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of disciplined practice and adherence to tradition as the foundation of spiritual life.

teachings.teaching: In Treta Yuga, the varnas perform their duties harmoniously, with each following the other.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of harmonious action (kriya) aligned with one's nature and role.

teachings.teaching: Kings arise to protect dharma and punish the wicked; Yama is the ultimate judge.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as the external manifestation of inner discipline: the king represents the will to uphold truth.

teachings.teaching: The Vedic mantras are self-manifested and revealed to the Saptarshis through their tapas.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's view that true knowledge arises from inner purification and meditation, not from external study alone.
